symbols: cross against open sky — the cosmic scope of Christ's atoning sacrifice, the axis mundi joining heaven and earth
angels catching the blood — the precious blood of Christ, the sacramental significance of his sacrifice
swooning Virgin — the sword piercing Mary's soul as foretold by Simeon (Luke 2:35)
Roman centurion gazing upward — the confession 'Truly this was the Son of God' (Matthew 27:54)
risen Christ in red robe — the resurrected Lord clothed in the glory of his victory over death
fallen soldiers at the tomb — the futility of earthly power against divine authority
diptych format — the theological unity of death and resurrection, the Paschal mystery as one event
description: A two-panel altarpiece in arched Gothic frames. The left panel depicts the Crucifixion: Christ hangs on the cross against a landscape background, flanked by two small angels catching his blood. Below, the Virgin Mary swoons supported by holy women, while a richly dressed centurion and soldiers stand to the right, a spear-bearer visible behind the cross. The right panel shows the Resurrection: Christ steps forth from the tomb wearing a luminous red robe, his right hand raised in blessing. An angel in white stands nearby, while armored soldiers lie sprawled and sleeping on the ground.
theological_description: This diptych presents the two inseparable poles of the Paschal mystery -- the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ -- as a single unified theological statement. The left panel captures the moment described in all four Gospels (Matt 27:33-56; Mark 15:22-41; Luke 23:33-49; John 19:17-37): the crucifixion at Golgotha. The angels reverently collecting Christ's blood emphasize the sacrificial-eucharistic theology developed in Hebrews 9:12-14, where Christ enters the holy places 'by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption.' The swooning Mary embodies the prophecy of Simeon in Luke 2:35 that a sword would pierce her soul. The right panel depicts the Resurrection (Matt 28:1-6), the foundational event of Christian faith. Paul's declaration in 1 Corinthians 15:17 -- 'if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile' -- is answered visually by the triumphant figure stepping from the tomb. The sleeping guards fulfill Matthew 28:4, where the soldiers 'shook and became like dead men.' The pairing of death and resurrection reflects the early Christian kerygma: 'He was crucified, died, and was buried; on the third day he rose again' (1 Cor 15:3-4). Christ's red garment in the Resurrection panel may allude to Isaiah 63:1-2, the one who comes from Edom with garments stained crimson -- a typological reading of the victorious Messiah.

scripture_refs.reason: The left panel directly depicts the Crucifixion at Golgotha with the mourning women, centurion, and soldiers described by Matthew.
The right panel shows the Resurrection with the angel at the tomb and the guards fallen as dead men.
The diptych format embodies the apostolic kerygma: Christ died, was buried, and rose on the third day.
The angels collecting Christ's blood signify the eternal redemption secured through his own blood.
The swooning Virgin at the cross fulfills Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce her soul.
Christ's red garment in the Resurrection panel may allude to the conquering figure from Edom in crimson robes.
